content.html: If you enable legacy_version_file it will read the version files used by other version managers (like .ruby-version) but I didn’t try the gemfile because I’ve only seen that once in a much older rails app so wasn’t aware it was a common pattern to add it there. It might!
content.text: If you enable legacy_version_file it will read the version files used by other version managers (like .ruby-version) but I didn’t try the gemfile because I’ve only seen that once in a much older rails app so wasn’t aware it was a common pattern to add it there. It might!

content.html: I didn’t use it for a long time bc of some poor reviews but I finally lost it with nvm and tried asdf for node and slowly began moving off all my other version managers. Then I discovered it could also version PG, elastic, and imagemagick and went all in and gave up rbenv
content.text:
I didn’t use it for a long time bc of some poor reviews but I finally lost it with nvm and tried asdf for node and slowly began moving off all my other version managers. 

Then I discovered it could also version PG, elastic, and imagemagick and went all in and gave up rbenv

content.html: I'm even running MySQL/PostgreSQL w/ asdf :)
content.text: I'm even running MySQL/PostgreSQL w/ asdf :)

content.html: Thanks! I’ve used the PG app before as well but very much like having everything under one roof. I also manage elastic, redis, and imagemagick with it 🔥

content.html: Not using postcss 8 would be guess #1 `yarn add -D tailwindcss@latest postcss@latest autoprefixer@latest` I’d also try nuking nodemodules and running yarn install bc sometimes that fixes weirdness.
content.text:
Not using postcss 8 would be guess #1

`yarn add -D tailwindcss@latest postcss@latest autoprefixer@latest`

I’d also try nuking nodemodules and running yarn install bc sometimes that fixes weirdness.
